todos.text: BUG: Haiku classifier returns "chat" for detailed multi-file feature specs (e.g. "Add a favourites system across the site..."). Prompt tightening alone did not fix it — Haiku over-applies the uncertainty fallback even when the request is unambiguously a feature. Options to investigate: (1) few-shot examples in the system prompt showing long feature specs classified as plan feature, (2) lowering the classification threshold by removing the uncertainty fallback entirely and defaulting to plan feature instead of chat when uncertain, (3) pre-screening prompt length/structure before sending to Haiku.

EXPLORE + ARCHITECT: Audit agent skills, tools, and pipeline composition for gaps and dead weight.

Run an EXPLORE pass first, then invoke the architect agent over the full agent system.

Findings from initial assessment (2026-04-02):

Gaps to address:
- gotcha-checker has no SKILLS.md section — fires every plan-stage STANDARD run but has zero domain guidance
- documenter has no SKILLS.md section — runs every apply cycle, no project-type context for what to record
- LEAN plan mode skips gotcha-checker entirely — too lean; gotcha-checker is cheap and should stay in LEAN
- No universal signals reference in SKILLS.md (reviewer-verdict, pipeline-summary, wave-complete formats only documented per-agent)

Low ROI candidates to evaluate:
- observer agent — manual invocation, log not surfaced in UI, overlaps with tool-call-auditor
- spec-agent, domain-context, tdd-agent — gated behind project.json flags nobody sets; add orchestrator branching with near-zero usage
- Several agents were retired in the April 2026 cleanup (nyquist-auditor, spec-agent, domain-context). Remaining audit scope: observer agent ROI, tdd-agent usage, gotcha-checker SKILLS.md gap

The architect agent should review the full .claude/agents/ directory, SKILLS.md, and templates/code/CLAUDE.md pipeline routing and produce a structured recommendation: what to add, what to retire, what to simplify.

FEATURE: Automatic intelligent module assignment for planned tasks.

When a planned item has no module assigned, automatically call an LLM (like enrich-todo) with the full modules.json structure + the feature title/description. The model decides:
- ASSIGN: feature belongs to an existing module as-is
- RESTRUCTURE: feature fits an existing module but its children need updating (rename, add, move capabilities)
- CREATE: feature genuinely warrants a new module (high bar — only when no existing module is even close)

Quality constraints the prompt must enforce:
- Not one module per feature
- No overlapping modules
- Proposed restructuring must not break existing children unless explicitly replacing them
- A CREATE response must justify why no existing module fits

The result is applied to modules.json automatically, and the planned item gets the module name set. If the model proposes RESTRUCTURE or CREATE, the changes to modules.json are also written.

Replace the existing manual 'suggested: X? CONFIRM' flow — this becomes fully automatic on promotion to PLANNED. The existing board task module-assignment-planned-create-new is superseded by this one.

BUG (PARKED): TODO→PLANNED promotion silently drops the task.

Three root causes were identified and fixed:
1. guessModule crash — mod.name undefined → added nullish coalescing
2. saveTerminalHistory IPC clone error — Svelte 5 proxy → added JSON round-trip
3. Board wiped after promotion — auto-save effect used wrong serialisation + getBoard reload overwrote with empty arrays → reverted to $state.snapshot + added guard

All three fixes are in the current code. Bug has not reproduced since early April 2026. PARKED — if it reappears, the race condition to investigate is: auto-save effect (App.svelte line ~59) firing between promoteTodoToPlanned() and the explicit saveBoard() call (line ~762), combined with the board reload (line ~850) reading stale disk data before the explicit save lands.

Key files: src/renderer/src/stores/project.svelte.ts (promoteTodoToPlanned), src/renderer/src/App.svelte (auto-save effect + onDone promotion + board reload sequencing).

FEATURE: Audit and update agent models — review every agent's assigned model, update LIVE tab and Settings agent tab to display model badges accurately.

Two parts:
1. Model audit: review each agent's model assignment in .claude/agents/*.md and confirm Sonnet vs Haiku assignments are correct for the role (e.g. triage agents on Haiku, heavy writers on Sonnet). Update any that are wrong.
2. UI: ensure the LIVE tab agent cards and the Settings > Agents tab both show the correct model name/badge per agent so the user can see at a glance which model is running.

FEATURE: Tone down TODO enrichment to Level 2 — rewrite for clarity without scope creep, 2–3 sentences max.

Current prompt says 'expand' and 'provide enough context that an AI agent can act without follow-up questions', which causes it to invent requirements (WCAG, hover states, JSON files, fuzzy matching) that the user never asked for.

New behaviour: clarify intent and add one scope boundary (what's NOT included), 1–2 sentences max. Do not add technical details, edge cases, or implementation choices not present in the original.

Change is in src/main/handlers/pipeline-data.ts — the `enrich-todo` IPC handler prompt string only.

COST: Route low-priority agents (documenter, cleanup) through cheaper execution paths. Concept: agents that run after apply and are not time-sensitive could use batch API or lighter models for ~50% cost savings. Scope TBD after restructure.

FEATURE: blockedBy support for board tasks. The data field already exists in board.json. Need: (1) MCP tool or signal to set/clear blockedBy, (2) /forge:status shows blocked tasks distinctly, (3) agents can emit [task-block] signal. Scope TBD after restructure.

UX GAP: In the PLANNED tab, each planned item has a MODULE input with an autocomplete datalist and a keyword-based suggestion ('suggested: X? CONFIRM'). But if no existing module fits, the user must type a freeform name that never gets linked to an actual module record in modules.json — it's an orphaned string. There is also no 'Create new module' affordance.

**What should change:**
(1) If the user types a module name that doesn't match any existing module in modules.json, show a 'CREATE MODULE' button inline that creates a minimal module record in modules.json with just a name (and empty capabilities[]) — so the feature ships into a real registered module, not a ghost string.
(2) After 'CREATE MODULE', the new name should immediately appear in the autocomplete datalist for all other planned items.
(3) If the user leaves MODULE empty and hits IMPLEMENT, show a soft warning: 'No module assigned — this feature won't appear in the module registry. Continue?' (non-blocking, can be dismissed).

**Also:** At TODO stage there is no module concept at all — this is intentional (TODOs are raw ideas). Module assignment only becomes relevant at PLANNED stage, which is correct. No change needed there.

FEATURE: Git integration for FORGE plugin. Auto-commit, branch-per-feature, optional PR creation via hooks or MCP tools. On apply start create feature branch, after implementer completes auto-commit, on success optionally create PR via gh CLI. Expose git operations as MCP tools or trigger via hooks. Closes competitive gap with Aider and Cursor.

EXPLORE: FORGE has a hard dependency on the Claude CLI and its stream-json output format. If Anthropic changes the CLI, stream-json schema, Task tool behavior, or pricing — FORGE breaks with no fallback. Explore: (1) map all the coupling points in runner.ts and shared.ts that are Claude-specific (Task tool, stream-json event types, --resume, --agents flags); (2) could FORGE detect CLI version and warn on breaking changes? (3) what is the minimum abstraction layer that would let FORGE swap the CLI without rewriting runner.ts? (4) how much of the agent behavior depends on Claude-specific capabilities vs standard tool-use patterns? Goal: understand the blast radius of a CLI change and find the cheapest way to reduce it.

EXPLORE/DISCUSS: How should FORGE support multiple AI models beyond Claude CLI? Current options to evaluate: (1) PATH-BASED — add a field in FORGE settings where the user pastes the path to another CLI executable (e.g. codex.exe, gemini-cli, gpt-engineer). FORGE spawns it the same way it spawns claude, passing prompt via stdin and reading stream-json or plain stdout. Requires each CLI to support a compatible output format — unlikely without adapters. (2) ADAPTER LAYER — build a thin adapter per model that translates FORGE's stream-json expectations to/from that model's output format. More work but model-agnostic. (3) API-DIRECT — call model APIs directly from the main process (no CLI dependency). Removes the Claude CLI dependency entirely but requires API key management and loses Claude's built-in tool use (Task, Read, Glob, etc.). (4) MODEL PER AGENT — let each agent declare which model it uses in its frontmatter (already supported in .md agent files). Budget agents use haiku, reviewer-safety uses opus. Evaluate which approach gives the most value with the least complexity. The PATH-BASED approach for a second CLI is the quickest win if the target CLI supports similar output.

FEATURE: Automated test execution in the apply pipeline. After implementer applies changes, detect project test runner from project.json, run tests via Bash or MCP tool, surface failures and optionally trigger debug pipeline. Opt-in toggle, 60s timeout, emit [suggest] debug rather than auto-fixing.

CONTENT: docs/BACKSTAGE.md currently covers 5 pipeline decisions (gotcha-checker order, planner questions, two gates, researcher order, reviewer-logic timing). Expand it with more entries as FORGE matures — good candidates: why apply feature is a separate pipeline from implement feature, why the coder never touches source files, why reviewer-safety always runs (non-demotable), why EXPLORE mode is read-only, why chat mode skips agent injection, why wave execution is opt-in, why the plan validity rule exists, why DIRECT mode has a redirect protocol. Add entries whenever a non-obvious design decision is made or a user asks why does it work this way.

EVALUATE: Agent registry overview — Claude Code has native /agents command that lists available agents. Evaluate whether this is sufficient or if FORGE needs a custom /forge:agents skill that shows pipeline role, model, mode membership, and invocation stats. May be unnecessary if native /agents gives enough info. Scope TBD after restructure.
CLEANUP: Consolidate plugin docs/ folder. Remove or merge stale docs carried over from Electron era. Ensure docs/ contains only what the plugin needs. Scope TBD after restructure.

DISCUSS: Pretend you are the developer of a competing AI dev tool (e.g. get-shit-done, aider, cursor, or similar) evaluating FORGE for the first time. Give an honest external assessment: (1) STRENGTHS — what would genuinely impress you compared to your own tool? Think: gate system, pipeline structure, specialised agents, signal protocol, wave execution, skills system, the fact that FORGE develops itself; (2) WEAKNESSES — what would make you uncomfortable or unconvinced? Think: token cost, pipeline complexity, orchestrator reliability, onboarding friction for new users, dependency on Claude CLI, no test automation, single-user focus; (3) MISSING — what features does your tool have that FORGE clearly lacks? (4) OVER-ENGINEERED — what would you call unnecessarily complex for the value it delivers? (5) VERDICT — would you be worried about FORGE as a competitor, and why or why not? Be harsh. The goal is to find real gaps and blind spots before real users do.

DESIGN: Multi-step request chaining — natural language requests that require multiple pipeline steps (e.g. "mark this done and add a follow-up task", "plan this then implement it"). Decide whether /forge:chat handles chaining or if a separate orchestration layer is needed. Scope TBD after restructure.

False positive plan-stage BLOCK traceability: tool-call-auditor only runs after apply pipelines, so false positive BLOCKs in plan pipelines (e.g. reviewer-logic reading stale handoff.md) are not audited. Options: (1) run tool-call-auditor after plan pipelines too, or (2) write a structured entry to audit-log.jsonl when PLAN-BLOCK-ESCALATED fires or orchestrator diagnoses a false positive. verdicts.jsonl captures the BLOCK verdict but not the why.

REFACTOR: Fold regression-risk logic into reviewer-triage and delete the standalone agent.

regression-risk is a separate Haiku agent that reads modules.json + handoff.md, identifies high-risk modules touched by the change, and emits [health] warnings. Nobody acts on those warnings — they sit in the HEALTH tab disconnected from reviewer dispatch.

Fold the logic into reviewer-triage: when triage reads the handoff to decide which reviewers to dispatch, also read modules.json and check which modules are touched. If high-risk modules are involved (shared utilities, IPC handlers, stores), promote reviewer-logic and/or reviewer-safety dispatch regardless of mode. The warning becomes an action.

After fold: delete .claude/agents/regression-risk.md. Remove from SCAFFOLD_AGENT_NAMES in shared.ts. Remove from pipeline agent lists in constants.ts and templates/code/CLAUDE.md.

Affected: .claude/agents/reviewer-triage.md (add module risk logic), .claude/agents/regression-risk.md (delete), src/main/shared.ts (SCAFFOLD_AGENT_NAMES), src/renderer/src/lib/constants.ts (PIPELINES agent lists).

IMPROVEMENT: Trigger reviewer-triage based on reviewer count, not mode — so LEAN runs that pick up a contextual reviewer still get triage when it pays off.

**The gap:** The routing table ties triage to mode (LEAN=no triage, STANDARD=triage). But contextual reviewers (reviewer-logic, reviewer-performance, reviewer-style) can be added to a LEAN run based on task signals. When that happens, N=3 reviewers all read the full handoff independently — past the break-even point where triage saves tokens, but triage still doesn't run.

**The fix:** After contextual reviewers are resolved and the final reviewer list is known, check the count. If N≥3 (regardless of mode), inject reviewer-triage before dispatching. Each reviewer then reads a focused excerpt instead of the full handoff. Mode stays as declared — no auto-promotion, no user surprise.

**Where to change:** `templates/code/CLAUDE.md` — in the `implement feature:` and `debug:/refactor:` pipeline sections, add an orchestrator rule: 'After resolving the reviewer list, if len(reviewers) >= 3 and mode is not FULL (FULL already includes triage unconditionally), invoke reviewer-triage before dispatching reviewers.'

**Why not mode promotion:** Promoting LEAN → STANDARD changes the user's declared mode, which could pull in completeness-checker and other STANDARD-only agents unexpectedly. Count-based triage is surgical — it only adds the token-saving coordination layer, nothing else.

**Break-even:** Triage costs ~1 full handoff read. It saves (N-1) full handoff reads by giving each reviewer a focused excerpt. Break-even is N=2; profitable at N≥3.

FEATURE: Add version field and migration logic to project.json.

project.json has grown organically — techStacks, agentSlots, references, pipelineMode, testerMode, enrichLevel. When multi-engine config lands (engine field, agentEngines overrides), existing projects need migration. Add:
1. "version": 1 field to project.json (current implicit schema)
2. A migrations array in shared.ts: each entry is { from: N, to: N+1, migrate: (json) => json }
3. On read, check version and run pending migrations automatically
4. On write, always stamp current version

First migration (v1 → v2): add engine: "claude" default. Future migrations handle schema changes without breaking existing projects.

Affected: src/main/shared.ts (migration runner), src/main/handlers/project-json.ts (version check on read/write), templates/code/.pipeline/project.json (add version to template).

FEATURE: Fetch and store project references — URLs, PDFs, and local files become persistent project knowledge.

Core flow: user adds a reference (URL, PDF path, or local file) → FORGE fetches/reads the content → extracts text → saves as clean markdown to docs/references/<slug>.md in the project. Agents and the orchestrator can read these files like any project doc.

New IPC: fetch-reference handler. Accepts { type: url|pdf|file, source: string, name: string, projectFolder: string }. For URLs: WebFetch + HTML-to-markdown extraction. For PDFs: text extraction (pdf-parse or similar). For local files: copy and convert to markdown. Output capped at 50KB per reference — warn if source is larger, offer to fetch specific sections.

Storage: docs/references/<slug>.md with frontmatter: source URL/path, fetch date, size, type. Managed list in .pipeline/project.json references array (already exists — extend with fetchedAt, contentPath, sizeBytes fields).

Affected: new handler src/main/handlers/references.ts, src/main/index.ts (register), src/preload/index.ts, src/renderer/src/lib/ipc.ts, src/renderer/src/types/claude.d.ts.

FEATURE: Multi-format reference ingestion — handle URLs, PDFs, and local files.

URL handling: fetch HTML, strip navigation/headers/footers, convert to clean markdown. Use a lightweight HTML-to-markdown approach (regex-based or a small library). Handle common doc sites: ReadTheDocs, Swagger/OpenAPI, GitHub READMEs, generic HTML.

PDF handling: extract text from PDF files. Options: (1) pdf-parse npm package (pure JS, works in Electron main process), (2) spawn a CLI tool, (3) Claude vision on PDF pages. Recommend pdf-parse for simplicity.

Local file handling: .md files copied as-is, .txt files wrapped in markdown, .docx needs a converter (mammoth npm package or similar).

All outputs: clean markdown, frontmatter with source metadata, capped at 50KB. If source exceeds cap, truncate with a note: "[Content truncated at 50KB — original source has N bytes. Fetch specific sections for complete coverage.]"

Affected: src/main/handlers/references.ts (format-specific extraction functions).

IMPLEMENTED: Supported formats: URL (HTML→text), .md, .txt, .html/.htm, .json (pretty-printed), .csv, .yaml, .xml, and other text files (used as-is). PDF, docx, images, and binaries return clear error messages telling the user to convert first. No external dependencies needed — all handled natively.

CHANGE: FULL pipeline mode should use reviewer-triage instead of skipping it. Currently FULL = all 5 reviewers unconditionally with no triage. Triage doesn't reduce quality — it provides file/line citations that make reviewers more focused and effective, and can still dispatch all 5 when warranted. New behaviour: FULL = triage runs with a floor of all 5 reviewers (triage annotates focus areas but cannot skip any reviewer). Saves tokens when a change genuinely doesn't touch a reviewer's domain while improving reviewer accuracy via targeted citations. Affects: CLAUDE.md pipeline mode table, agent prompts for reviewer-triage, and any orchestrator logic that checks mode to decide whether to run triage.

DOCS: Document enterprise backend compatibility. Plugin runs wherever Claude Code runs — if Claude Code supports Azure/Bedrock/Vertex, FORGE inherits it. Write FAQ for enterprise users. Scope TBD after restructure.

BUG: Reviewer subagent failed to read docs/PLAN.md during plan-stage review — file exists on disk (verified) but the agent reported 'does not exist'. Happened twice: first the original reviewer BLOCKED with 2 blockers but empty output file, then the re-run couldn't find PLAN.md at all. Likely a path resolution issue in the subagent context — the agent may be resolving relative paths from a different working directory, or the Read tool is failing silently. Also affects reviewer-safety which couldn't read triage excerpt files (separate but related). Investigate: how subagents resolve working directory, whether docs/PLAN.md path works from the agent's perspective, and why output files are empty when verdicts are emitted.

BUG: Default agent-roles.json for new projects only allows implementer to write to src/**. Projects that create files in config/, tests/, data/, or other top-level directories get blocked mid-apply and require manual patching. Seen on Diesel priser project — implementer couldn't write config/settings.json or tests/**. Fix: widen the default allowed paths in the scaffolded agent-roles.json to include config/**, tests/**, data/**, and any other common project directories. Affects: templates/*/agent-roles.json scaffold, .pipeline/agent-roles.json in FORGE root (copied to new projects).

BUG: In LEAN mode, reviewers expect triage excerpt files that don't exist. LEAN mode runs reviewer-safety + reviewer directly (no triage step), but the reviewer agents look for docs/context/triage-excerpts/*.md which is only produced by reviewer-triage in STANDARD/FULL mode. Reviewer-safety failed on Diesel priser Fetch plan because of this — had to be manually re-invoked. Fix: reviewer agent prompts should not require triage excerpts; they should fall back to reading PLAN.md or handoff.md directly when no excerpt exists.

EFFICIENCY: Multiple agents read the same file 3-7 times per run. Observed patterns: (1) completeness-checker reads handoff.md 4-5 times, (2) boundary reviewer writes reviewer.md 3-5 times per run (same content), (3) tool-call auditor reads audit log 3-7 times. These are token-wasting anti-patterns that the agent-optimizer should address. Root cause is likely agent prompts that re-read files they already have in context, or retry loops that re-read on each attempt. Fix: audit agent prompts for redundant read patterns and add explicit 'read once, cache in context' instructions.

AGENT FIX: Add Bash tool to implementer agent. Currently implementer has Read/Write/Edit/Glob/Grep but no Bash — can't run mkdir, build commands, or verify changes. Seen in pipeline runs where implementer can't create directories. Affects: .claude/agents/implementer.md frontmatter.

AGENT FIX: Replace Bash tool usage in reviewer-triage with Write tool. Triage currently uses `cat > file << 'EOF'` via Bash to write excerpt files, which is fragile on Windows (caused failures in pipeline runs). Should use Write tool directly. Affects: .claude/agents/reviewer-triage.md — change tools from Bash to Write, update excerpt writing instructions.

REFACTOR: Merge regression-risk agent into reviewer-triage. Both read handoff.md + modules.json. Regression-risk flags risky modules, triage dispatches reviewers — these should be one agent. Saves one agent spawn per implement run. Affects: .claude/agents/reviewer-triage.md (add module risk analysis), .claude/agents/regression-risk.md (delete), constants.ts (remove from pipeline lists).

CLEANUP: Delete or archive dead/unused agents: implementer-triage (now wired — keep), tester (always skipped per user preference), observer (manual-only, unused), tdd-agent (never enabled). Tester and observer could be archived to .quarantine/ rather than deleted.

EFFICIENCY: Drop coder-scout from LEAN mode pipeline. The scout reads the plan, greps for files, and writes scout.json — but the coder already reads the plan and has the same tools. In LEAN mode (simple features), the scout adds a full agent spawn for information the coder discovers in its first 3-4 tool calls. Saves ~10-15s + ~5k tokens per implement run. Affects: PIPELINE_MODE_AGENTS in constants.ts (remove coder-scout from lean), orchestrator LEAN routing.

EFFICIENCY: Skip completeness-checker in LEAN mode. With 5-8 tasks, the coder either hits them all or the reviewer catches gaps. Across 3 real pipeline runs (Diesel priser), completeness-checker approved instantly every time — never found anything. Saves ~8-12s + ~4k tokens per implement run. Affects: PIPELINE_MODE_AGENTS in constants.ts (remove completeness-checker from lean).

EFFICIENCY: Merge cleanup agent into documenter — one agent pass instead of two. Cleanup checks two conditions (RESEARCH file exists, PLAN-archive > 500 lines) and does simple file ops. The documenter already reads the same files and could do this inline at the end of its run. Saves one agent spawn (~10s + ~3k tokens) per apply run. Also eliminates cleanup's redundant handoff.md re-read. Affects: .claude/agents/documenter.md (add cleanup steps), remove cleanup agent invocation from apply pipeline.

[DROPPED — native compaction] EFFICIENCY: Cache agent context across runs in the same session. Agents re-read GENERAL.md, CLAUDE.md every invocation even though they do not change. Use ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA} or session state to avoid redundant reads. Saves 10-30k tokens per run. Scope TBD after restructure.

DESIGN: Move Q&A clarifying questions from the planner into the One Chat orchestrator, before the approach proposal. Current flow: orchestrator proposes pipeline → user approves → planner asks questions → writes plan. Problem: planner questions can reveal the task is simpler or more complex than the orchestrator assumed, but by then the pipeline/mode are locked in. New flow: orchestrator asks clarifying questions first (in conversation) → user answers → orchestrator proposes pipeline/mode/agents with full context → user approves → planner receives answers and skips Q&A. Trade-off: adds one round-trip before approach, but produces better pipeline decisions. Architectural change to One Chat flow — touches ORCHESTRATOR_RULES, planner agent prompt, QaStrip integration.

FEATURE: Knowledge compounding step — after each apply pipeline, capture the solution in a structured docs/solutions/ directory with YAML frontmatter (problem_type, module, tags, files_touched). Future planner/coder runs search these via a learnings-researcher pattern. Inspired by Compound Engineering Plugin. This replaces flat DECISIONS.md as the searchable knowledge store. Agents read it during plan/implement to avoid repeating past mistakes and reuse proven patterns.

FEATURE: Knowledge maintenance — periodic command that scans docs/solutions/ against current codebase. Flags stale docs (reference deleted/renamed files), consolidates duplicates, archives outdated solutions. Prevents knowledge rot. Inspired by Compound Engineering compound-refresh.

FEATURE: Ideate step — proactive codebase analysis that suggests high-impact improvements. Goes beyond architect HEALTH mode by being adversarial (what is wrong, what could be better, what is risky). Emits [todo] signals for suggested improvements. Can run as explore-mode command or as optional pre-planner step. Inspired by Compound Engineering ideate.

FEATURE: Scope guardian — add a scope-creep check to the gotcha-checker or as a new lightweight agent. Asks: is this plan trying to do too much? Does the scope match the original request? Catches oversized plans before they enter the review cycle. Inspired by Compound Engineering scope-guardian-reviewer.

FEATURE: Past session search — search run history for patterns and solutions. Have we seen this error before? What worked last time? Store run history in ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}, searchable by agents during plan/debug. Inspired by Compound Engineering session-historian. Scope TBD after restructure.

[PARKED] FEATURE: Context7 MCP integration — add @upstash/context7-mcp as a tool available to researcher and coder agents for live framework documentation lookup. Covers 1000+ libraries. Free, no API key. Replaces unreliable WebSearch for framework-specific questions. Spawn as MCP server process or call REST API directly. [SPLIT into sub-tasks] FEATURE/DESIGN: Parallel pipeline sessions with git worktree isolation.

The killer feature: start task B while task A is still running. Each pipeline run gets its own git worktree so file changes dont conflict. User switches between sessions to check progress.

How it works:
1. User starts a pipeline (plan feature, implement, etc.)
2. FORGE creates a git worktree for the task
3. Pipeline runs in the background (Agent tool with run_in_background)
4. User immediately starts another task in a new session
5. A dashboard command shows all running sessions with progress
6. User can expand any session to see live agent progress (like Claude Code native agent viewer)
7. Completed sessions merge back via git

Plugin model enables this naturally — multiple terminal windows, each its own Claude Code session, sharing worktrees. Electron model is limited to one CLI process.

Design questions:
- How does the session dashboard work? /forge:dashboard reads run-active.json markers across worktrees?
- How do we handle the merge step? Auto-merge on completion, or manual?
- Should sessions share the same board.json or each have their own?
- How do we prevent two sessions from planning against the same stale codebase?
- Agent viewer: expandable cards showing per-agent progress (tool calls, status) — like Claude Code native but integrated

This reshapes how FORGE works — from sequential (wait for pipeline) to parallel (start and move on). Strongest argument for the plugin migration.

FEATURE: Dashboard live-update and health signals. The sidecar dashboard (scripts/dashboard-server.mjs) is shipped with: HTML UI, active runs, gates, recent completions, board summary, welcome/help panel, gate approve/discard, merge-blocked retry/discard, auto-refresh via 5s client polling, in-session launch from /forge:dashboard. Remaining scope: (1) WebSocket or SSE push to replace polling — reduces latency and unnecessary network traffic; (2) health signal rendering — surface [health] signals from pipeline runs in the dashboard UI.

SECURITY: Red-team audit of FORGE. We've never actively attacked our own plugin and there are several classes of dumb-but-serious oversights we could easily have shipped. Scope a proper adversarial review, ideally using multiple reviewer-safety passes in different red-team framings — credential leakage, supply chain, input handling, process spawning, path boundaries, distribution surface. Non-exhaustive threat list to cover explicitly:

1. **Secret leakage** — grep the repo and history for `sk-`, `ghp_`, `gho_`, `api_key`, `token`, `secret`, `password`, `bearer`, common cloud key prefixes (`AKIA`, `AIza`, etc.). Include `docs/`, `templates/`, `.pipeline/`, every committed file + recent git history. Check `hooks/mcp-deps-install.js`, `mcp/server.js`, `mcp/lib/*.js`, `forge-config.default.json`, `.mcp.json` for any hardcoded keys. We know `CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA` is used for config but verify no plaintext fallback exists. The existing agent-roles + `envVar`-only pattern for OpenAI keys should be re-audited end-to-end.

2. **Command injection** — every `execSync`, `execFileSync`, `spawn`, `pty.spawn` call. Look at `hooks/mcp-deps-install.js` (npm install shell-out), `bin/forge-worktree.js` (git), `hooks/gate-sync.js` (worktree creation), `scripts/forge-wrapper-proto.mjs` (Claude PTY + `where`/`which`). User-controlled input reaching any of these through feature names, run IDs, project paths, or config values.

3. **Path traversal / arbitrary write** — anywhere hooks or MCP tools write files based on user-controlled strings. `forge_create_run` uses run IDs; `forge_update_task` writes board items; `workflow-guard.js` validates worktree paths for apply runs but maybe not for all runs. Check that run IDs, feature names, and file paths can't escape `.pipeline/` or the worktree.

4. **Hook script untrusted input** — every hook reads a JSON payload on stdin from Claude Code. If an attacker controlled that JSON (unlikely but verify), what could they do? `workflow-guard.js` is the critical one — gate approval carries security weight.

5. **Agent prompt injection** — agents receive user-provided task text (via board) and file content (via Read). Can a malicious TODO or file trigger dangerous subagent behaviour? Scope: can a `[todo]` or `[suggest]` signal be forged to poison the board or agent-optimizer? Can `[reviewer-verdict]` be impersonated?

6. **Supply chain** — `.claude-plugin/marketplace.json`, `package.json`, `mcp/package.json`, `packages/forge-core/package.json`. FIX: Stale `forge-config.json` at `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}` — bootstrap never updates existing live configs, so changes to `forge-config.default.json` never reach users who installed FORGE before the change. Router silently routes to stale models based on the outdated catalog.

**Evidence (verified 2026-04-18):**
- `hooks/mcp-deps-install.js` `bootstrapForgeConfig()` copies `forge-config.default.json` to `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}/forge-config.json` ONLY on first run — the `if (existsSync(targetPath)) return;` guard blocks all subsequent updates.
- Live config verified via `forge_list_models providerId=gemini availableOnly=true`: returned a single model `gemini-2.0-flash` (caps: reasoning, code, analysis, fast), which was explicitly REMOVED from the default in commit `fe4c822` ("Removed deprecated gemini-2.0-flash from the catalog — its quota was 0 and alphabetical tiebreak was silently selecting it first").
- Missing from live: `gemini-2.5-flash`, `gemini-2.5-flash-lite`, `gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview`, `gemini-2.5-pro`, `gemini-3.1-pro-preview`.
- Downstream: `forge_get_model_recommendation` for `reviewer-safety` with caps `[code, analysis]` returned `claude-haiku-4-5-20251001` (3-cap low-tier). With an up-to-date catalog, `gemini-2.5-flash` (3-cap free-tier) should have won the cheapest-tier tiebreak — a free-tier call was silently lost to a paid Haiku call on every reviewer dispatch today.
- AgentModelMap is also stale — `reviewer-safety` router reason says "satisfying [code, analysis]" while default specifies `[reasoning, code, analysis]`. `reviewer-boundary` returned source `"default"` ("No routing requirements declared"), implying its agentModelMap entry is missing entirely in the live config.

**Required fix — migration mechanism:**

1. **Diff-aware migration in `hooks/mcp-deps-install.js` `bootstrapForgeConfig()`** (or a sibling function run every SessionStart):
   - If live config is missing → bootstrap from default (existing behavior).
   - If live config exists → compute a diff against the default and MERGE:
     - Add new `models[]` entries (by `id`) that exist in default but not in live.
     - Remove `models[]` entries (by `id`) that exist in live but not in default (retired models like `gemini-2.0-flash`).
     - Update `capabilities[]`, `costTier`, `pricing{}`, `contextWindow`, `notes` for models where the default has newer values — PRESERVE user overrides only if they add fields the default doesn't have.
     - For `agentModelMap[]`: same merge rules. New agents added, removed agents dropped, capability lists updated to default.
     - For `providers[]`: keep user's `enabled`/`priority`/`envVar` overrides; update `name`/`type`/`notes` from default.
   - Invalidate the module-level config cache after merge (`mcp/lib/config-store.js` `invalidateConfigCache()`).

2. **Version / schema tracking:** add a `schemaVersion` or `defaultsRevision` field to `forge-config.default.json` and live config. Migration runs when these differ, idempotent when they match. Avoids running the merge on every session when nothing changed.

3. **Safety rails:**
   - Back up the existing live config to `forge-config.json.bak-<timestamp>` before writing the merged file.
   - Log the diff (added/removed/updated) to stderr so the user sees what migrated.
   - On merge failure: leave live config untouched, log the error, continue session (fail-open — routing falls back to whatever the stale config has, not worse than today).

4. **User-scoped override preservation:**
   - Identify and preserve any model IDs OR agent names that exist ONLY in the live config (user added, not from default). Do not remove.
   - For field-level merges, user edits to `notes` or `costTier` on a default-sourced model stay unless the default explicitly bumps the value. Conservative: when in doubt, keep user value.

**Scope breakout (suggest splitting into two slices):**
- SLICE A (small, ship first): a one-shot "catalog refresh" — remove retired Gemini models, add the 5 missing ones. One-off fix for the current drift. Can be a manual script run once OR a bootstrap-flag-file approach.
- SLICE B (larger): the proper migration mechanism above. Generalizes SLICE A to handle all future drift automatically.

**Pipeline:** LEAN (reviewer-safety + reviewer-boundary mandatory; this touches config correctness). SLICE B may warrant STANDARD.

**Testing:** manually verify `forge_list_models providerId=gemini availableOnly=true` returns the expected 4+ models after migration; re-run `forge_get_model_recommendation` for `reviewer-safety` and confirm `gemini-2.5-flash` is now selected.

**Reference:** 2026-04-18 session audit surfaced this; see the stale-flag quota fix (`hooks/usage-clear-quota-flags.js`) as adjacent precedent for "config that never auto-heals" anti-patterns.

FEATURE: PreToolUse hook `hooks/gate-enforcement.js` that mechanically enforces Gate #1 and Gate #2 approvals on Agent-tool dispatches. Prevents the "collapse Gate into status line" failure mode that happened live on 2026-04-18 (observer-launcher slice and forge-config-migration slice — reviewer verdicts reported and implementer dispatched in the same turn with no user pause).

**Mechanism:**
- Matches `Agent` tool on PreToolUse. Parses `tool_input.subagent_type`.
- If `subagent_type === 'forge:implementer'`: read `.pipeline/gate-pending.json` (or worktree-local variant when set), require `status === "approved"` for the current feature. If absent or pending → exit 2 with stderr blocker message.

- Mode-aware carve-out: read `.pipeline/project.json` for `pipelineMode`. If mode is `TRIVIAL` or `SPRINT`: exit 0 regardless (those modes bypass gates by design).
- Feature-name binding (optional but valuable): require the implementer prompt's feature name matches the gate-pending.json feature field; prevents "approved gate for feature A, dispatched implementer for feature B" mistakes.
- Fail-open on I/O errors (missing gate-pending.json is treated as "not approved" — that's fail-SAFE, not fail-open; the block is the correct outcome).

- `hooks/routing-enforcement.js` already uses this pattern to block Agent spawns without a prior `forge_get_model_recommendation` call. Same structure: PreToolUse matcher `Agent`, exit 2 on block.

BUG FIX: Stale `run-active.json` pointer pollutes agent logs across sessions.

**Root cause (verified 2026-04-18):**
- `.pipeline/run-active.json` is the "pointer to the currently-executing formal pipeline run." It's written by `forge_create_run` / `forge_resume_run`, updated by `hooks/subagent-start.js` on every SubagentStart event.
- When a pipeline ends without explicit teardown (crash, abandonment, session close mid-run, manual discard without clearing the pointer), `run-active.json` keeps pointing at the terminal run indefinitely.
- `hooks/subagent-start.js` lines 104-110 appends EVERY dispatched FORGE subagent to whatever run the pointer names, with no freshness / status / lifecycle check. It trusts the pointer unconditionally.
- Result: today's 30+ subagent dispatches across 6 unrelated slices got logged to a 3-day-old `r-d1afe1f3` "red-team security audit" plan run. Pollutes `forge_get_active_run`, `forge_dashboard_state`, the statusline, `/forge:resume`, and any analysis that reads `run-active.json.agents[]`.
- `hooks/ctx-session-start.js` DOES clear stale `currentUnit` when the referenced run is terminal — but it doesn't apply the same guard to the run-level pointer. Asymmetric defensive coverage.

**Two design gaps to fix:**

**Gap 1 — SubagentStart hook has no lifecycle guard.**
- `hooks/subagent-start.js` should read the pointed-at run's status from the registry (`.pipeline/runs/<runId>/run.json`) before appending.
- If the run is terminal (completed / failed / discarded) → skip the append; optionally log a one-line stderr note (`[forge-subagent] skipping append to terminal run <runId>`).
- If the run is running / gate-pending / created → proceed with append as today.
- If the registry entry is missing → fail-open (treat as running), same as today.

**Gap 2 — no SessionStart cleanup for stale run-active pointer.**
- Add logic to `hooks/ctx-session-start.js` (or a new sibling SessionStart hook) that extends the existing `emitStaleUnitNoticeIfAny()` pattern: if `run-active.json`'s referenced run is terminal, NEUTRALIZE the pointer by deleting the file (or writing an empty `{}` shell). Same defensive check as currentUnit, applied to the whole run pointer.

**Scope — LEAN slice, 3 files:**
1. EDIT `hooks/subagent-start.js` — add status lookup + terminal guard before pushing to agents array.
2. EDIT `hooks/ctx-session-start.js` — extend stale-check to neutralize run-active.json when the referenced run is terminal.
3. EDIT `docs/gotchas/GENERAL.md` — document the pointer-lifecycle contract.
4. EDIT `docs/CHANGELOG.md` — entry.

**Agent team:** coder → reviewer-safety (mandatory — filesystem writes, potential for breaking run lifecycle) → reviewer-boundary (mandatory — must not break `forge_create_run` / `forge_resume_run` / `currentUnit` contract from `packages/forge-core/src/runs/index.js`) → implementer → documenter.

**Pipeline:** LEAN.

**Known limitation to flag in slice:** subagents dispatched OUTSIDE formal pipelines (e.g. from conversational Claude manually calling `Agent(subagent_type: "forge:...")` without a `forge_create_run` wrapper) will still have nowhere to log. That's actually desirable — conversational dispatches shouldn't pollute formal-run agent arrays. Current behavior writes them anyway because the pointer exists; the fix drops them on the floor when the pointer is terminal. A followup might add a "conversational run" concept for these, but that's a separate feature not a bug fix.

**Reference:** 2026-04-18 session-cleanup incident; see existing patterns in `hooks/ctx-session-start.js` `emitStaleUnitNoticeIfAny()` (the terminal-check template) and `hooks/usage-clear-quota-flags.js` (the session-scoped-clear template).

2. Skill files may not instruct the orchestrator to pass the model through at all — check skills/plan/SKILL.md, skills/implement/SKILL.md, etc. "Model routing" sections.

DESIGN: LEAN pipeline token burn is not "lean" — reviewers run by default even when they add no signal.

**Observed 2026-04-19 (plan run r-96162b4a + implement run r-373dd6f6):**

Concrete token evidence from this session's tool results:

| Stage | Tokens | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Planner | 30,906 | 6 tool calls |
| Reviewer-safety (plan) | 32,300 | 11 tool calls, verdict: APPROVED, no findings |
| Reviewer-logic (plan) | 29,560 | 8 tool calls, verdict: APPROVED, no findings |
| Reviewer-boundary (plan, ×3 dispatches) | 94,158 | 2 failures + 1 success, verdict: APPROVED, no findings |
| Coder | 47,542 | 18 tool calls, clean handoff with self-review |
| **Estimated total so far** | **~234,506** | Pre-Gate #2, pre-apply |

User feedback: "A lean pipeline shouldnt cost anything close to the amount of tokens we just used — ESPECIALLY it shouldnt run reviewers for the sake of just running reviewers. It isnt 'LEAN' at this point. Supervisor/executer feels like it is doing just as good work without the token burn"

**Root causes (hypotheses to verify):**

1. **Every reviewer independently re-reads the same files.** Each reviewer reads: its own agent definition (~100 lines), handoff.md (~230 lines), GENERAL.md (huge), 2-3 source files (~350 lines combined). Three reviewers × same reads = 3× waste. Triage excerpt files (`docs/context/triage-excerpts/reviewer-*.md`) exist for this but are populated only in STANDARD/FULL modes, not LEAN.

2. **LEAN-mode reviewer set ignores handoff self-review.** The coder's handoff already includes a "Self-review" block (handoff.md lines 212-224) that walks async/state/edge-cases/return-checks. LEAN pipeline dispatches reviewer-safety + reviewer-boundary anyway, even when the coder's self-review has already covered the same ground.

3. **No "trivial-diff skip" gate.** 5-task LEAN fixes on ~15 lines of code (like this one) dispatch the same reviewers as a 50-line security-sensitive change. The pipeline has no mechanism to look at the handoff diff size and decide "this doesn't need reviewer X."

4. **Reviewer-boundary specifically is burning tokens to fail.** TODO `89dab759` already tracks that reviewer-boundary emitted no verdict across 3 dispatches in this run — ~94k tokens for one effective APPROVED.

**Design questions to answer:**

1. **Should LEAN mode default to SKIP reviewers?** If the coder writes a self-review, is a separate reviewer dispatch net-negative value for small surgical diffs? Argument for: reviewer catches model blind spots. Argument against: reviewers running on haiku are at the same capability tier as the coder — they're not "more expert," just a second pass.

2. **Supervisor/executor parity check.** User observes the Supervisor pattern (Gemini-backed single-call via `forge_call_external`) produces equivalent work at a fraction of the tokens. Why? Because: (a) context is injected once, not re-read by each agent; (b) no orchestration overhead from multi-turn subagent dispatch; (c) single-model accountability. Formal comparison needed — run the same feature through both paths and measure tokens.

3. **Reuse triage excerpts in LEAN.** Currently `docs/context/triage-excerpts/reviewer-*.md` are populated only in STANDARD/FULL (via reviewer-triage agent). Even in LEAN, producing a small triage excerpt (just the diff + GENERAL.md pointer) before dispatching reviewers would halve their reads.

4. **Diff-size heuristic.** If `handoff.md` diff section is <30 lines of code, treat it as `SPRINT` (core + no reviewers) regardless of user's configured LEAN floor — with operator override.

5. **Handoff self-review as verdict substitute.** If the coder's handoff includes an explicit "Self-review" block addressing safety + boundary + logic branches, downstream reviewer dispatch may be redundant for LEAN mode. Needs validation: compare coder self-review verdicts against reviewer verdicts across ~10 runs.

**Fix scope (to be planned):**

1. Add diff-size measurement to implement skill — count non-doc lines changed in handoff.md.
2. Introduce an opt-in "LEAN-strict" vs "LEAN-lite" distinction, where LEAN-lite skips reviewers when coder self-review is present and diff is surgical.
3. Populate triage excerpts in LEAN mode too — not just STANDARD/FULL.
4. Compare Supervisor-pattern (single `forge_call_external` with injected context) end-to-end token cost vs multi-reviewer LEAN dispatch on 3-5 real features. Document findings.
5. Consider: make reviewer dispatch default-off in LEAN, opt-in via flag or explicit mention ("add safety review" in the user prompt). Reviewer-safety + reviewer-boundary are "mandatory for any source change" per CLAUDE.md — revisit that constraint in light of self-review.

**Agent team (when planned):** researcher (compare-path token measurement) → planner → coder → reviewer-safety → implementer → documenter. Scope: STANDARD or FULL — this touches the pipeline design itself.

- Existing triage excerpt map: `docs/gotchas/GENERAL.md` "Context injection map" section.

BUG: `bin/forge-worktree.js merge` gives up on any conflict — defeats its purpose as the auto-merge helper.

**Observed 2026-04-19 (apply run r-5a0218c4, worktree r-373dd6f6):**

The helper is supposed to be the "press button, worktree merges back to main" automation. In practice it failed the first real test.

**What happened (verbatim from Bash tool output this turn):**

1. First invocation: `node bin/forge-worktree.js merge r-373dd6f6`
   Output: `{"ok":false,"error":"Merge failed — conflicts or diverged branches. Worktree and branch preserved for manual resolution.","branch":"forge/r-373dd6f6","into":"main","worktreePath":".worktrees\\r-373dd6f6","hint":"Resolve manually: git merge forge/r-373dd6f6"}`

2. The `"hint": "Resolve manually"` response was accurate — but the user shouldn't have to resolve manually. The whole point of the helper is to do the git dance.

3. Diagnosis via `git merge --no-commit --no-ff forge/r-373dd6f6` revealed:
   - `CONFLICT (content): Merge conflict in docs/PLAN.md`
   - `CONFLICT (content): Merge conflict in docs/context/handoff.md`
   Root cause: both branches modified those files. Main had the planner's edits from the plan pipeline; worktree had documenter's updates (tasks marked `[x]`) and the coder's fresh handoff.

4. Manual fallback: `git merge -X theirs forge/r-373dd6f6 -m "feat(forge): fix stale run-active.json pointer pollution"` succeeded: `Merge made by the 'ort' strategy. 7 files changed, 251 insertions(+), 300 deletions(-)`.

**Why this matters:**

- The `/forge:apply` skill explicitly calls this helper as Step 9 "Worktree merge-back (automatic when a worktree-backed implement run exists)". The word "automatic" is aspirational — any conflict breaks it.
- Conflicts on `docs/PLAN.md` and `docs/context/handoff.md` are NOT edge cases — they are the NORMAL outcome whenever the plan pipeline writes to main's docs/ BEFORE the implement worktree is created, and the documenter then edits the same files in the worktree. That's the default flow.
- The user correctly called this out: "the merge helper was made to do this for us, so we didn't have to do it manually."

**Root cause hypotheses (to verify by reading `bin/forge-worktree.js`):**

1. The helper probably invokes `git merge <branch>` with no `-X` strategy. For two files that were independently-edited on both sides, `ort`/`recursive` cannot auto-merge without guidance and bails.
2. No staged fallback — on conflict detection, the helper returns early instead of attempting `-X theirs` (or `-X ours`) as a second pass.
3. No file-specific strategy — `docs/PLAN.md` and `docs/context/handoff.md` are predictable conflict sites; they have well-defined ownership (documenter writes to PLAN in worktree; coder writes to handoff in worktree). A `.gitattributes` merge driver or an explicit per-file resolution in the helper would eliminate this class entirely.
4. No pre-merge clean-tree check — a `git diff-index HEAD --quiet` on main before attempting merge would at least surface "you have uncommitted changes on main" BEFORE the merge fails with the opaque message I got first (`"local changes would be overwritten"`).

**Fix scope (to be planned):**

1. **Two-pass merge strategy.** First pass: plain `git merge`. On conflict: second pass with `-X theirs` (worktree is authoritative for files the pipeline wrote to — PLAN.md, handoff.md). Only fall back to "resolve manually" if the second pass also fails.
2. **Pre-merge hygiene check.** Before attempting merge, run `git status --porcelain` on main. If there are uncommitted changes in files the worktree branch also modified, surface a clear error BEFORE the merge attempt: "Main has uncommitted changes on <files>. Commit, stash, or discard before merging."
3. **File-specific merge drivers.** Register a gitattributes merge driver for `docs/PLAN.md` and `docs/context/handoff.md` that always prefers the worktree version (these are pipeline-owned artefacts, not hand-edited source files). This eliminates the conflict class entirely without needing `-X theirs`.
4. **Better error surface.** The current `{"ok":false,"error":"Merge failed — conflicts or diverged branches"}` doesn't tell the operator WHICH files conflicted. Dump `git status -s` output into the error payload so the root cause is visible without a second manual diagnosis command.
5. **Optional: auto-resolve-and-report.** After successful two-pass merge, emit a summary of which files were auto-resolved and which strategy won — operator visibility without manual inspection.

**Agent team (when planned):** coder → reviewer-safety (git operations on shared branch are safety-sensitive — no `--force`, no `--no-verify`) → reviewer-logic (two-pass merge state machine; must not double-merge or leave dirty tree) → implementer → documenter. Scope: LEAN.

**Forbidden operations to preserve:** `--force`, `--force-with-lease`, `--amend`, `--no-verify`, `git reset --hard`, `git clean`, `git stash drop`. The fix must not introduce any of these — already enforced by the apply skill's "Forbidden operations" list.

**Reference:** 2026-04-19 apply run r-5a0218c4; worktree r-373dd6f6. First-pass failure message captured verbatim above. Manual resolution commit hash is the merge commit on main from this session.
